body:
The Illusion of Control in the Dreamscape

Sleep researcher Rubin Naiman speaks of the modern world’s war on sleep, a battle waged with stimulants, screens, and a relentless obsession with productivity. But this war extends into the very fabric of our inner worlds. We don’t just fight sleep; we fight our dreams. We arrive in the dreamscape armed with agendas, demanding answers, seeking control, and attempting to wrestle the wild, untamable mystery of the soul into submission. We treat our dreams like unruly employees, expecting them to perform on command and deliver neat, actionable insights. This is the ego’s folly, a desperate attempt to manage the unmanageable. The dream world is not a boardroom; it is a vast, untamed wilderness, and it does not answer to your meticulously crafted five-year plan. The moment you try to control a dream, you have already lost its true gift. The practice of dream surrender is not about giving up; it is about giving over. It is the conscious, courageous act of relinquishing the illusion of control and allowing the dream to lead. It is a radical act of trust in a world that teaches us to trust nothing we cannot measure, quantify, or dominate. We are a culture addicted to certainty, terrified of the unknown. We want our spirituality sanitized, our healing linear, and our mysteries solved. We want the soul to behave, to conform to our expectations, to fit neatly into the boxes we have built for it. But the soul is not a domestic pet. It is a wild god, and it will not be tamed. This relentless drive for control has turned our inner worlds into another project. Conditioned to measure our worth by output, we bring this frantic energy to our dreams, seeking a measurable return. We have forgotten how to simply be, to rest in the unknown, and trust our own becoming.

When we love, we feel one with everything.

What Is Dream Surrender?

Dream surrender is the intentional practice of entering the dream state with a posture of radical openness. It is the conscious decision to release all expectations, agendas, and desires for a particular outcome. It is not passivity, but an active, engaged receptivity. Think of it as the difference between trying to capture a wild animal and sitting quietly in the forest until it chooses to reveal itself. One is an act of force, the other an act of invitation. In my years of working in this territory, I've seen that the most profound dream encounters happen not when we are hunting for meaning, but when we create a space for meaning to find us. This practice requires a profound shift in orientation, from doing to being, from seizing to receiving. It means entering the waters of the unconscious without a map, trusting that the currents will carry you exactly where you need to go. It is the understanding that the dream’s wisdom is not a prize to be won, but a grace to be received. It is a deep bow to the intelligence of the soul, an acknowledgment that your conscious mind, with all its striving and scheming, knows so very little. It is a practice of profound vulnerability, of letting go of the need to know, the need to be in charge, the need to have it all figured out. It is the willingness to be humbled, to be surprised, to be undone. It is the quiet confidence that even in the darkness, even in the confusion, you are held. This practice requires a willingness to feel your full emotional spectrum, to sit with the discomfort of not knowing, and to face your disowned parts. It is a practice of radical self-acceptance, welcoming all that arises in the dreamscape without judgment.

For deeper exploration of this work, Kalesh offers personal guidance and consciousness teaching that builds on these foundations.

What's posing as 'love' is often just depression, self-denial, or emotional manipulation dipped in patchouli.

The Arrogance of Dream Interpretation

The modern spiritual marketplace is flooded with dream dictionaries and self-proclaimed gurus who promise to decode your dreams for a fee. They offer a seductive promise: that the vast, multidimensional mystery of your soul can be neatly categorized and explained. This is not just a disservice; it is an act of spiritual violence. To reduce a dream to a set of pre-approved symbols is to strip it of its living essence. It is to trade a universe for a shoebox. A dream is not a rebus puzzle waiting to be solved. It is a living, breathing encounter with the divine. It is the soul’s poetry, and you cannot diagram a poem without killing it. The arrogance lies in the assumption that the conscious mind is the ultimate arbiter of meaning. It is the belief that we, with our limited, conditioned intellects, can stand in judgment of the dream. The practice of dream surrender invites us to a place of profound humility. It asks us to approach the dream not as experts, but as students. It asks us to sit with the dream, to feel its textures, to listen to its silences, and to allow its meaning to unfold in its own time, in its own way. It is a move from interpretation to communion. This commodification of the sacred is a symptom of a deeper sickness, a spiritual materialism that seeks to possess and consume even the most intangible aspects of our being. We want to buy our enlightenment, to download our healing, to outsource our spiritual labor. But the soul cannot be bought. It cannot be hacked. It cannot be optimized. It can only be met, in the raw, messy, unpredictable reality of the present moment. We have become spiritual tourists, collecting experiences like souvenirs, but never allowing them to change us. We want transformation without the mess, but the soul's path is a wild, unpredictable journey into the mystery.

Gabor Maté's work on the connection between trauma and the body illuminates why these dreams carry such physical intensity.

Go play. Go deep. Be fully and unapologetically YOUR TRUE SELF.

How to Practice Dream Surrender

The practice of dream surrender begins before your head hits the pillow. It begins with the cultivation of a surrendered attitude in your waking life. It is the practice of noticing your attachment to outcomes, your need to be right, your fear of the unknown. As you prepare for sleep, create a ritual of release. This might involve journaling, meditation, or simply a few moments of quiet intention-setting. Consciously release the day’s events, the worries of tomorrow, and any and all expectations for your dream life. You might use a simple mantra, such as: "I release my need to control. I open myself to the wisdom of my dreams." As you drift into sleep, hold this intention of openness. When you awaken, whether in the middle of the night or in the morning, resist the urge to immediately analyze or dissect the dream. Instead, simply lie with it. Feel its emotional residue in your body. What sensations are present? What is the lingering feeling-tone? Before your mind rushes in to label and categorize, simply be with the experience. Record the dream in a journal without interpretation. Capture the images, the dialogue, the feelings, the raw data of the dream. Then, let it rest. Throughout the day, carry the dream with you. Allow it to percolate in your awareness. Notice any synchronicities or insights that arise. The dream’s meaning will often reveal itself not in a flash of intellectual understanding, but in a subtle shift of perception, a new feeling, a deeper sense of connection to your own life. This is a practice of patience, of trusting the slow, organic unfolding of wisdom. It is a practice of honoring the mystery, of allowing the soul to speak in its own language, in its own time. Creating a sacred space for your dreams, a small altar with a candle or a special stone, signals your readiness to listen.

I welcome all light-beings, awakened masters and positive energetic constructs into my life as partners in the pursuit of clarity, light and love.

Better to be called dramatic, angry, or difficult than to die as a ghost of your true self.

The Dark Side of Surrender: When Letting Go Becomes Giving Up

There is a shadow side to the practice of surrender, a place where letting go can curdle into a passive resignation. This is not true surrender, but a spiritual bypass, a way of avoiding the difficult, messy work of doing the work for our own lives. True surrender is not about giving up your agency. It is not about becoming a doormat to the universe. It is about aligning your will with a will greater than your own, about becoming a conscious co-creator with the divine. The distinction is crucial. Giving up is an act of despair. Surrender is an act of trust. Giving up is a collapse. Surrender is an opening. Giving up is a retreat from life. Surrender is a deeper engagement with it. If you find yourself using the language of surrender to justify your inaction, to avoid difficult conversations, to stay in situations that are draining your life force, then you are not practicing surrender. You are practicing avoidance. The practice of dream surrender is not a call to abdicate your responsibility. It is a call to a deeper form of responsibility, a responsibility to the soul, to the truth, to the wild, untamable mystery of your own being. It is the courage to say “yes” to the unfolding of your life, even when it is difficult, even when it is painful, even when it leads you into the heart of the fire. It is the willingness to be an active participant in your own liberation, to take the risks, to make the choices, to do the work that is required to become the person you were born to be.

The Fruits of Surrender

The paradox of dream surrender is that in giving up the search for answers, we open ourselves to a much deeper form of knowing. When we cease our striving, we create a space for grace to enter. The fruits of this practice are not the neat, tidy insights that the ego craves. They are far more subtle, and far more profound. You may find a deepening of your intuition, a greater sense of trust in the flow of your life. You may experience a sense of being held and guided by a wisdom far greater than your own. You may find a new level of intimacy with your own soul, a feeling of coming home to yourself. The American Psychological Association has published numerous articles on the benefits of dreamwork for mental health, but the practice of surrender takes this a step further. It is not just about psychological integration; it is about spiritual communion. It is about remembering that you are not a lone sailor on a vast and empty ocean, but a wave in the ocean of consciousness, inseparable from the whole. It is about dissolving the illusion of the separate self and resting in the vast, loving embrace of the mystery. This is not a path for the faint of heart. It is a path for those who are willing to be undone, to be broken open, to be remade in the image of a love that is fierce, wild, and utterly unconditional. You may find that your creativity blossoms, that your relationships deepen, that your sense of purpose becomes clearer. You may find that you are more able to navigate the challenges of your life with grace and resilience. You may find that you are less afraid of death, and more in love with life.

For more on the psychology of dreaming, you can explore resources from the American Psychological Association.

May all beings give and receive love. May all the beings in all the realms, in all the worlds, be happy.

Frequently Asked Questions

What if I can't remember my dreams?

The inability to remember dreams is often a symptom of the very control we are seeking to surrender. It can be a form of unconscious resistance, a fear of what the dream might reveal. Begin by setting a clear intention to remember your dreams before you go to sleep. Keep a journal by your bed and write down anything you can recall upon waking, even if it’s just a fragment or a feeling. The act of honoring these small pieces sends a powerful message to your unconscious that you are ready to listen. Keep going. Even when it feels pointless.; the practice is about the intention, not the immediate result. It is also important to examine your relationship with rest. Are you allowing yourself enough time for deep, restorative sleep? Or are you treating sleep as another task to be optimized and controlled? The dreams will come when they feel safe to emerge. Consider also your diet and lifestyle. Are you consuming stimulants like caffeine or alcohol close to bedtime? Are you engaging with screens late into the night? All of these factors can interfere with your ability to remember your dreams. Create a sleep sanctuary, a space that is dark, quiet, and cool. Make your bedroom a haven for rest and rejuvenation, not another office or entertainment center.

Is it ever okay to try to influence my dreams, like with lucid dreaming?

Lucid dreaming can be a powerful practice, but it is a different practice than dream surrender. Lucid dreaming is about waking up within the dream and consciously participating in its creation. Dream surrender is about remaining in a state of receptive allowing. Both have their place. However, if you find yourself using lucidity as another form of control, another way to avoid the uncomfortable truths the dream may be trying to show you, then it is no longer a spiritual practice but an egoic game. The key is the intention behind the practice. Are you seeking to dominate the dream, or to dance with it? Are you seeking to impose your will, or to collaborate with the will of the soul? The dream is not a playground for the ego. It is a temple for the soul. If you choose to explore lucid dreaming, do so with a spirit of curiosity and respect. Use your lucidity not to control the dream, but to engage with it more deeply. Ask questions of the dream characters. Explore the dream environment with a sense of wonder and awe. Use your conscious awareness to be more fully present to the mystery that is unfolding.

Boundaries without backbone are just polite requests to be violated again.

What if my dreams are nightmares?

Nightmares are the soul’s cry for attention. They are a sign that there is something in your unconscious that is demanding to be seen, felt, and integrated. To practice surrender with a nightmare is a profound act of courage. It means being willing to stay with the terror, to feel the fear, and to ask the terrifying image what it has come to teach you. It is not about re-traumatizing yourself, but about meeting the disowned parts of yourself with compassion and curiosity. Often, when we turn and face the monster in our dreams, it transforms into a powerful ally. The nightmare is not your enemy. It is your teacher. It is pointing you toward the places within yourself that are in most need of your love, your attention, your presence. If you are struggling with recurrent nightmares, it may be helpful to seek the guidance of a therapist or spiritual director who is experienced in dreamwork. They can provide a safe and supportive container for you to explore the deeper meaning of your dreams and to integrate the powerful energies that they contain.

The Unfinished Business of The Practice of Dream

The practice of dream surrender is not a one-time fix. It is a lifelong orientation, a continual returning to a state of openness and trust. It is the unfinished business of the soul. Every night, you are offered an invitation to let go of the small, constricted world of the ego and to enter the vast, boundless expanse of the mystery. Every morning, you are asked to bring the wisdom of that journey back into your waking life. This is not a practice for the faint of heart. It requires a willingness to be undone, to be humbled, to be remade. It asks you to trade the illusion of certainty for the reality of a living, breathing relationship with the divine. The spiritual path is not about arriving at a final destination. It is about the courage to continue the journey, to keep surrendering, to keep opening, to keep becoming. What part of you is still clinging to the shore, afraid to enter the water? What would it take for you to finally let go and trust the current? The dream is calling. The soul is waiting. The mystery is here, now, in this very breath. Are you ready to answer? The invitation is always open. The door is always unlocked. The choice, as always, is yours.

I ask my higher Self to rise above thought and mind so that I can live and behave in union with the ever-blossoming eternal consciousness.

And please, PLEASE bring on lots of women. Women are better than men in just about every area.

faqs.question: Can recurring dreams about this theme indicate unresolved trauma?
faqs.answer: They can, but not always. Recurring dreams indicate that a message has not been received. Sometimes that message is about trauma. Sometimes it is about growth your conscious mind is resisting. The repetition is the unconscious saying: you have not heard me yet.

faqs.question: Should I be concerned if these dreams are disturbing?
faqs.answer: Disturbing dreams are not the problem — they are the alarm system. The unconscious escalates intensity when gentler messages have been ignored. A disturbing dream is your psyche saying: this matters enough to make you uncomfortable.

faqs.question: Can two people share the same dream experience?
faqs.answer: Shared dreaming is reported across cultures and throughout history. Whether you frame it as telepathy, collective unconscious, or coincidence depends on your worldview. What matters is what the shared dream is communicating to each person individually.

faqs.question: How do I distinguish between anxiety dreams and meaningful ones?
faqs.answer: Anxiety dreams tend to recycle the same emotional pattern with different surface content. Meaningful dreams introduce new elements — new characters, new settings, new emotional textures. But here is the uncomfortable truth: anxiety dreams are meaningful too. They are telling you something about your nervous system that you need to hear.
